title.en: Zero-Shot Image Classification with CLIP: Vision That Understands Language

description.en: Use OpenAI's clip-vit-base-patch32 to classify images with nothing but plain-English labels â€” no training data, no fine-tuning â€” then apply the same trick to build text-to-image search over a photo library.

story_variants.en:
## The Model That Sees With Words

Vaathiyaar slides a photograph across the desk. "Classic image classifiers are like students who memorized exactly one exam. Train a model on 1,000 ImageNet categories and it recognizes those 1,000 â€” ask about anything else and it shrugs. **CLIP** broke that ceiling. OpenAI trained it on 400 million imageâ€“caption pairs from the internet, teaching it one skill: *match pictures to text*. The result? Describe any category in plain English and CLIP can recognize it â€” with **zero** training examples. That's zero-shot classification."

### Two Encoders, One Shared Space

CLIP is really two models glued together. A **Vision Transformer** (the `vit-base-patch32` part â€” it slices images into 32Ã—32 patches) encodes images into 512-dimensional vectors. A **text transformer** encodes sentences into the *same* 512-dimensional space. During training, matching imageâ€“caption pairs were pulled close together and mismatched pairs pushed apart. So classification becomes geometry: encode the image, encode each candidate label, and the closest label wins.

### Zero-Shot vs. Traditional Classification

| Property | Traditional classifier | CLIP zero-shot |
|---|---|---|
| Training data needed | Thousands of labeled images | None |
| Classes | Fixed at training time | Any text, changeable at runtime |
| Add a new class | Retrain the model | Just add a string |
| Size | Varies | ~600 MB, runs on CPU |
| Best accuracy | Higher on its exact classes | Strong across open-ended classes |

### Classification in a Few Lines

```python
from transformers import CLIPModel, CLIPProcessor
from PIL import Image

model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")
processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")

image = Image.open("mystery.jpg")
labels = ["a photo of a dog", "a photo of a pizza", "a photo of a mountain"]

inputs = processor(text=labels, images=image, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
probs = model(**inputs).logits_per_image.softmax(dim=1)
```

Notice the labels aren't bare words â€” they're phrased as **"a photo of a ..."**. CLIP saw captions during training, so prompts that read like captions score better. This *prompt engineering for vision* is free accuracy.

### From Classification to Photo Search

Flip the direction and you get search. Encode every photo in a folder once with `get_image_features`, store the vectors, then encode a query like *"birthday cake on the beach"* with `get_text_features` and rank photos by cosine similarity:

```python
img_vecs = model.get_image_features(**processor(images=photos, return_tensors="pt"))
query_vec = model.get_text_features(**processor(text=["birthday cake on the beach"], return_tensors="pt", padding=True))
```

That's the entire engine behind text-to-image search â€” the same one Homie uses to search your local photo library without sending a single pixel to the cloud.

> **Key Insight:** CLIP puts images and text into one shared 512-dimensional vector space, turning classification into a similarity lookup. Your "classes" are just strings you can change at runtime â€” and the same embeddings that classify an image also power text-to-image search. One model, two superpowers, no training data.

explanation.en: CLIPModel holds both encoders; CLIPProcessor prepares images and text for them
PIL loads image files â€” CLIP accepts plain PIL images
Download the model once (~600 MB), then it's cached locally. Runs fine on CPU.
The processor resizes images to 224Ã—224 and tokenizes text â€” both inputs, one helper
Any photo works â€” CLIP has never seen it and needs no training on it

steps.line:
labels = [
    "a photo of a beach",
    "a photo of a city street",
    "a photo of a forest",
    "a photo of a kitchen",
]

explanation.en: Classes are just strings! The 'a photo of a ...' template matches CLIP's caption-style training data
Preprocess the image and tokenize all four labels into PyTorch tensors in one call
Both encoders run: the image and every label land in the same 512-dim space
logits_per_image holds imageâ†”text similarity scores; softmax turns them into probabilities

steps.line:
for label, p in zip(labels, probs[0]):
    print(f"{p:.1%}  {label}")

explanation.en: The beach label dominates â€” classified with zero training examples

output: 3.1%  a photo of a city street

animation_sequence.message: You just classified an image with nothing but words. Zero-shot vision unlocked!

instruction.en: Build a zero-shot image classifier. Complete `classify` so it takes a PIL image and a list of text labels, runs CLIP, and returns a dict mapping each label to its probability. The demo creates a solid red image â€” CLIP should confidently pick the red label over the blue one.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import torch
from transformers import CLIPModel, CLIPProcessor
from PIL import Image

model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")
processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")

def classify(image, labels: list[str]) -> dict[str, float]:
    """Return {label: probability} for the image, probabilities summing to 1."""
    # 1. Preprocess: processor(text=labels, images=image, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
    # 2. Run the model inside torch.no_grad()
    # 3. Softmax outputs.logits_per_image along dim=1
    # 4. Zip labels with the probabilities (use .item() for floats)
    pass

img = Image.new("RGB", (224, 224), color=(220, 30, 30))
result = classify(img, ["a photo of a red square", "a photo of a blue square"])
print(max(result, key=result.get))
practice_challenges.expected_output: a photo of a red square

en: processor(text=labels, images=image, return_tensors="pt", padding=True) prepares both inputs at once
outputs.logits_per_image has shape (1, num_labels) â€” apply .softmax(dim=1) and read row [0]
Build the dict with {label: prob.item() for label, prob in zip(labels, probs[0])}

practice_challenges.test_code:
import torch
from transformers import CLIPModel, CLIPProcessor
from PIL import Image

_model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")
_processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")

def classify(image, labels):
    inputs = _processor(text=labels, images=image, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = _model(**inputs)
    probs = outputs.logits_per_image.softmax(dim=1)[0]
    return {label: p.item() for label, p in zip(labels, probs)}

img = Image.new("RGB", (224, 224), color=(220, 30, 30))
labels = ["a photo of a red square", "a photo of a blue square"]
result = classify(img, labels)

assert set(result.keys()) == set(labels), "Must return one probability per label"
assert abs(sum(result.values()) - 1.0) < 1e-4, "Probabilities must sum to 1 (use softmax)"
assert all(0.0 <= p <= 1.0 for p in result.values()), "Each value must be a probability"
assert max(result, key=result.get) == "a photo of a red square", "CLIP should pick the red label for a red image"
print("Zero-shot classification tests passed!")

instruction.en: Power the search side of a CLIP photo library. Complete `embed_texts` so it encodes a list of strings with CLIP's text encoder into unit-normalized vectors, and `similarity` so it returns the cosine similarity between two texts. Related captions must score higher than unrelated ones â€” the exact ranking logic Homie uses for text-to-image search.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import torch
from transformers import CLIPModel, CLIPProcessor

model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")
processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")

def embed_texts(texts: list[str]) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Return an (N, 512) tensor of unit-normalized CLIP text embeddings."""
    # 1. Tokenize: processor(text=texts, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
    # 2. Encode with model.get_text_features(**inputs) inside torch.no_grad()
    # 3. Normalize each row: vecs / vecs.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    pass

def similarity(a: str, b: str) -> float:
    """Cosine similarity between two texts (dot product of normalized vectors)."""
    pass

close = similarity("a dog playing on the beach", "a puppy running in the sand")
far = similarity("a dog playing on the beach", "a spreadsheet of tax records")
print(round(close, 3), round(far, 3))

en: model.get_text_features(**inputs) returns raw (N, 512) embeddings â€” the text-only half of CLIP
After normalizing rows to unit length, cosine similarity is just a dot product
In similarity(), embed both texts together and return float(vecs[0] @ vecs[1])

practice_challenges.test_code:
import torch
from transformers import CLIPModel, CLIPProcessor

_model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")
_processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")

def embed_texts(texts):
    inputs = _processor(text=texts, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
    with torch.no_grad():
        vecs = _model.get_text_features(**inputs)
    return vecs / vecs.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

def similarity(a, b):
    vecs = embed_texts([a, b])
    return float(vecs[0] @ vecs[1])

vecs = embed_texts(["hello world"])
assert vecs.shape == (1, 512), "CLIP text embeddings must be 512-dimensional"
assert abs(vecs[0].norm().item() - 1.0) < 1e-4, "Embeddings must be unit-normalized"

close = similarity("a dog playing on the beach", "a puppy running in the sand")
far = similarity("a dog playing on the beach", "a spreadsheet of tax records")
assert close > far, "Related captions must score higher than unrelated ones"
print("Text embedding search tests passed!")
